questions.question: Explain the Waterfall Model of software development with its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Sequential approach with distinct phases
Phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Maintenance
Advantages: Simple to understand, clear milestones, easy to manage
Disadvantages: Inflexible to changes, late testing, high risk

questions.question: What are functional and non-functional requirements? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Functional requirements describe what the system must do
Non-functional requirements describe system attributes
Example functional: User should be able to login
Example non-functional: System should respond within 3 seconds

questions.question: Describe the four types of software maintenance with brief examples.
keyPoints: Corrective: Fixing bugs/errors
Adaptive: Modifying for new environment
Perfective: Improving performance/usability
Preventive: Making changes to prevent future problems

questions.question: Explain the concept of coupling and cohesion in software design. Why are high cohesion and low coupling desirable?
keyPoints: Coupling: degree of interdependence between modules
Cohesion: degree to which elements within a module are related
High cohesion: focused, maintainable modules
Low coupling: easy to modify, test, and reuse modules

questions.question: List and briefly explain the different levels of software testing.
keyPoints: Unit testing: testing individual components
Integration testing: testing combined components
System testing: testing complete integrated system
Acceptance testing: validating against user requirements

questions.question: What is the Spiral Model? Explain its key features and when it is most appropriate to use.
keyPoints: Risk-driven iterative model
Combines waterfall and iterative approaches
Four phases: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Evaluation
Best for large, complex, high-risk projects

questions.question: Discuss the key principles of Agile methodology.
keyPoints: Iterative and incremental development
Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
Working software over comprehensive documentation
Responding to changes over following a plan
Adaptive planning and continuous improvement

questions.question: Explain the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) in detail. Describe each phase with its inputs, outputs, and key activities.
keyPoints: Requirements Analysis: gathering user needs, creating SRS document
System Design: creating architecture and design documents
Implementation: actual coding of the system
Testing: verifying functionality through various test levels
Deployment: installing software in production environment
Maintenance: ongoing support and updates
Each phase has specific inputs, activities, and outputs

questions.question: Compare and contrast Waterfall, Spiral, and Agile software development models. Discuss when each is appropriate to use.
keyPoints: Waterfall: sequential, rigid, good for well-defined requirements
Spiral: risk-driven, iterative, good for large complex projects
Agile: flexible, collaborative, good for changing requirements
Waterfall disadvantages: inflexible to changes
Spiral advantages: risk management, flexibility
Agile advantages: customer satisfaction, adaptability
Selection criteria: project size, requirements stability, risk level, team size

questions.question: What is Software Quality Assurance (SQA)? Explain the SQA activities and how they contribute to producing quality software.
keyPoints: SQA is a systematic process for ensuring software quality
Activities include: quality planning, quality assurance, quality control
Quality planning: define quality standards and procedures
Quality assurance: process-focused activities to prevent defects
Quality control: product-focused activities to detect defects
Benefits: early defect detection, consistent quality, customer satisfaction
Includes testing, reviews, audits, and process improvement

questions.question: Explain the concept of requirements engineering. Discuss the process of requirements gathering, analysis, specification, and validation.
keyPoints: Requirements engineering: systematic approach to defining requirements
Requirements gathering: techniques like interviews, surveys, observation
Requirements analysis: feasibility study, conflict resolution, prioritization
Requirements specification: documenting in SRS format
Requirements validation: reviewing for completeness and correctness
Challenges: changing requirements, communication gaps, stakeholder conflicts
Importance: prevents rework, ensures user satisfaction, reduces costs
